coredata.dc:title: Orange emissive N-doped carbon dots and their application in detection of water in organic solvents and the polyurethane composites
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Jingjing
Cui, Jingjing
Ma, Yidan
Zhao, Wenhao
Wang, Yulong
Li, Zhenzhong
coredata.dc:description: In this work, efficient orange emissive N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized through a facile one-step solvothermal route using 1,4-phenylene diisocyanate as the only raw precursor. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited strict excitation independent luminescence with a maximum emission peak at 581 nm. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield was up to 38%. A reasonable formation process of N-CDs was explored in detail. Interestingly, the N-CDs was sensitive to water and thus, could be exploited as an excellent fluorescent probe directly for quantitative detection of water in organic solvents. Moreover, various orange-emitting N-CDs/polymer composites were achieved by simply incorporating the N-CDs into different polymer matrices. The N-CDs could be better uniformly dispersed in polyurethane (PU) matrix through the in situ polymerization technique, and the emission intensity of N-CDs/PU composite films could be linearly modulated by changing the N-CDs concentration. These investigations broaden promising application potentials of CDs in sensor and optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Long-wavelength emission
Photoluminescence
Organic solvent
Water sensor
Nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of N-CDs and their photographs under daylight (left) and UV lamp (right).
(a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of N-CDs. (b) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs. (c) XPS full survey and (d–f) high-resolution XPS C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra of N-CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs (inset: the fluorescence image under optimal excitation). (b) Excitation-emission map of N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption (inset: the Abs spectrum of PPDI precursor), (b) PL spectra (inset: the corresponding QYs), (c) Fluorescence decays (inset: the fitting parameters), and (d) FTIR spectra of the samples with different reaction time.
(a) PL spectra and (b) PL emission peak of the same CDs in different solvents excited by 518 nm light.

(a) Emission spectra of N-CDs in DMF with different water contents. (b) Fluorescence intensity and (c) emission peak versus water content in DMF. (d–f) UV–vis absorption, FTIR and fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs solution in absence and presence of 25% water.
(a) Percent of transmittance (inset: the digital photographs under daylight) and (b) PL spectra (inset: the corresponding QYs) of the in situ and ex situ CDs/PU composites.

(a) Emission spectra of CDs/PU composites with different CDs concentration (inset: photographs of all samples under visible (top) and UV light (below)). (b) Mechanical properties and (c) TGA thermograms of pure PU and CDs/PU composite containing 9 mL CDs solution.
A possible formation process for N-CDs.
Comparison of the water detection in organic solvents among different CDs sensors.

head.ce:title: Orange emissive N-doped carbon dots and their application in detection of water in organic solvents and the polyurethane composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Engineering, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan, Shanxi, 030008, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Engineering, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan, Shanxi, 030008, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Frontiers Science Center for Flexible Electronics, Xi'an Institute of Flexible Electronics and Xi'an Institute of Biomedical Materials & Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an, Shaanxi, 710072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Frontiers Science Center for Flexible Electronics
Xi'an Institute of Flexible Electronics and Xi'an Institute of Biomedical Materials & Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Frontiers Science Center for Flexible Electronics, Xi'an Institute of Flexible Electronics and Xi'an Institute of Biomedical Materials & Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an, Shaanxi, 710072, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, efficient orange emissive N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized through a facile one-step solvothermal route using 1,4-phenylene diisocyanate as the only raw precursor. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited strict excitation independent luminescence with a maximum emission peak at 581 nm. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield was up to 38%. A reasonable formation process of N-CDs was explored in detail. Interestingly, the N-CDs was sensitive to water and thus, could be exploited as an excellent fluorescent probe directly for quantitative detection of water in organic solvents. Moreover, various orange-emitting N-CDs/polymer composites were achieved by simply incorporating the N-CDs into different polymer matrices. The N-CDs could be better uniformly dispersed in polyurethane (PU) matrix through the in situ polymerization technique, and the emission intensity of N-CDs/PU composite films could be linearly modulated by changing the N-CDs concentration. These investigations broaden promising application potentials of CDs in sensor and optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial, have drawn extensive attention for years [1 – 3]. Based on their unique and attractive merits, such as cost-effective preparation, great environmental sustainability, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, amazing photoluminescence, and abundant functional groups [4–6], CDs have been intensively studied for potentially wide applications in fluorescent sensors, bioimaging, photocatalysis, optoelectronic devices, etc [7 – 9]. Nowadays, CDs with various fluorescence emission colors have been successfully obtained, however, most of them emit short-wavelength fluorescence (e.g. blue or green light) [10 – 12]. Long-wavelength (e.g. orange and red light) luminescent CDs are still very scarce because the emissions theoretically need efficient large size of conjugated sp2-domains [13,14], which is relatively difficult to obtain and easily makes them unstable [15–17]. Such drawbacks severely hampered their development and applications [6,18], especially concerning their great importance in the fields of biorelated applications and warm white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) illumination systems [2,19]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop efficient orange or red emissive CDs materials.

By far, researchers have put a lot of effort into the achievement of long-wavelength emissive CDs. For example, Sun’ group synthesized red emissive CDs with a maximum peak at ∼600 nm, prepared from precursors citric acid and urea in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and the relative photoluminescence quantum yields (PL QY) reached 12.9% [18]. Wang et al. dissolved p-phenylenediamine in diphenyl ether and heated with reflux condensation system to synthesis CDs. The obtained CDs in different solvents could emit different long wavelength lights (green to red). The emission peak was observed at 602 nm in CH3OH, showing orange-red light with relative PL QY of 24.8% [4]. Qu et al. proposed surface metal-cation-functionalization strategy to prepare orange emissive CDs with optimal emission at 580 nm from citric acid and urea in DMF. The surface charges engineering was a benefit for efficient orange emission and the PL QY was as high as 46% [9]. Ge and co-workers obtained the precursor polythiophene derivatives via multiple reaction steps, and further prepared red emissive CDs with low PL QY of 2.3% [15]. Xiong's group achieved red-emitting CDs centered at 625 nm from p-phenyldiamine and urea. Silica column chromatography separation was carried out carefully to get the right fraction. The PL QY of the red emissive CDs was approximately 24% [16]. Miao et al. prepared S, N doped CDs with red emission using citric acid and thiourea as the resources through solvothermal method. The PL emissions exhibited excitation-dependent luminescent characteristics and the optimal emission located at ∼594 nm with an absolute PL QY of 29% in ethanol [6]. In brief, various methods, among reported CDs, have been adopted to realize the long-wavelength emissions, such as conjugated precursors [13,15], heteroatom doping [6,11], solvent engineering [4,19], surface modification [3,20], additive strategy [9,16], and so on. Although many decent orange and red emissive CDs have been obtained in these pioneering works, complicated preparation procedures, which are time-consuming and poor controllability, are always necessary [2,10]. Furthermore, most reported red-emitting CDs have the excitation-dependent phenomenon, which often delivered very low QYs for long-wavelength lights [2,21]. To the best of our knowledge, there are few reports about the achievement of highly efficient long-wavelength emissive CDs using a simple and facile route, which is of crucial importance in understanding both the formation process and the luminescence mechanism of CDs and, consequently, realizing efficient orange or red emissive CDs [2,16,19].

Herein, new orange emissive N-CDs was synthesized through a simple one-pot solvothermal method by using 1,4-phenylene diisocyanate (PPDI) as the only raw precursor (see Fig. 1 ). The as-prepared N-CDs emitted strong fluorescence with the maximum wavelength of 581 nm, and exhibited strictly excitation-independent luminescence with an absolute PL QY up to 38%. A reasonable formation process of N-CDs from PPDI was explored in depth. N-CDs could be dissolved in various solvents, and the optical property was sensitive to water which could cause apparent fluorescence quenching. On the basis of this finding, a sensitive fluorescent sensor was exploited for water contents in organic solvents, which constructs a fluorescent sensing platform for the on-site detection of water without any sophisticated processes. What's more, it is still a great challenge to realize efficient long-wavelength emission of CDs-based solid materials, because the solid-state luminescence quenching of CDs constitutes a severe issue [7,19]. Our as-synthesized N-CDs could be embedded in different polymer matrices to fabricate N-CDs/polymer composites without compromising their indigenous optical properties. Since PPDI is one of the common raw materials for PU, PU is an appropriate matrix for obtaining homogeneous functional composites. A series of orange-emitting N-CDs/PU composite films, which presented concentration dependent intensity, were prepared through the in situ synthesis. The facile preparation procedures and excellent orange luminescence of N-CDs under both solution and solid state make it an attractive candidate in sensor and photoelectric applications.

1,4-phenylene diisocyanate (PPDI), methylene-bis(4-cyclohexylisocyanate) (HMDI), 1,4-butanediol (BDO), and dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTDL) were received from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Poly(caprolactone) diol (PCL, M n = 1000 g/mol) was obtained from Daicel Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethyl acetate, tetrahydrofuran (THF), toluene, acetone, dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), trichloromethane (CHCl3), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), n-propanol, and n-butanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, China. PCL and BDO were dried in a vacuum oven at 110 °C for 2 h to remove residual water prior to use. All the other chemical reagents were used without further purification.

N-CDs were prepared from PPDI in a one-pot solvothermal process. In detail, PPDI (0.1 g) was dispersed in DMF solvent (10 mL). Then, the solution was transferred into a 25 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the resulting CDs solution was centrifuged to remove large particles.

CDs/PU composites were fabricated through in situ polymerization. Firstly, various amounts (0, 0.5, 1.5, 3, 6, 9, and 12 mL) of the corresponding CDs solution were diluted by DMF to 16 mL. PCL (1.247 g) was added with stirring to form a homogenous solution. HMDI (0.62 g) and a drop of DBTDL were imported sequentially, and the mixture was heated at 80 °C for 3 h to synthesize the prepolymer. Then, BDO (0.136 g) was introduced, and the reaction was continued for 0.5 h. Finally, the products were cast in the Teflon mold, followed by curing at 80 °C in an oven to obtain the CDs/PU composite films.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) observations were performed on a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired from a Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer (Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by PHI–5000C ESCA system (PerkinElmer Inc., USA). UV–vis absorption spectra and percent of light transmittance were collected on a Hitachi spectrophotometer (U-3900, Japan). Fluorescence spectra, quantum yields, and fluorescence lifetimes were measured by Edinburgh FLS-1000 spectrofluorometer (England). Tensile mechanical properties were determined by using a universal testing machine (Gotech, Qingdao, China) according to the GB/T 528–2009. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted on a TG 209F3 (Netzsch, Germany) with a heating rate of 10 °C/min from 30 °C to 600 °C under the nitrogen atmosphere.

In the solvothermal process, the reaction solvent plays a crucial role in the optical properties of CDs [2,22]. Various solvents were first tested experimentally. For the most solvents, the as-prepared solutions didn't fluoresce (Fig. S1). DMF was ultimately selected as the best solvent, because the product emitted a clear orange fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1). As proposed in previous studies [9,16,19], DMF could provide higher degree of dehydration and carbonization of CDs precursors, resulting in large sized conjugated sp2-domains, which is the basis of bandgap orange emission. Afterward, the concentration between PPDI and DMF was adjusted in parallel reactions, and it was found to be 0.1 g PPDI: 10 mL DMF for the strongest fluorescence (Figs. S2a and b). Similarly, the solvothermal temperature (Figs. S2c and d) and the reaction time (Figs. S2e and f) were optimized to be 160 °C and 3 h, respectively. TEM images of the obtained N-CDs depicted in Fig. 2 a reveals a quasi-spherical shape. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 2a) further identifies a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice distance of the graphitic carbon [2,10].

FTIR and XPS analyses were carried out to confirm the structure and element composition on the surface of N-CDs. In FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2b), many vibration bands are observed, such as O–H/N–H (3140–3700 cm−1), C–H (2924 and 2852 cm−1), CO/CN (1668 cm−1), CC (1514 cm−1), C–N= (1387 cm−1), and C–O (1101 cm−1). The results reveal that the N-CDs are composed of polyaromatic structures and possess abundant oxygen and amino containing functional groups on their surface [4,10]. XPS full spectrum (Fig. 2c) further demonstrates the existence of C, N, and O on the surface of the N-CDs, suggesting N heteroatom was successfully doped into the CDs. The atomic percentages of C: N: O are 84.27: 8.72: 7.03. Comparing to the theoretically calculated atomic ratio of PPDI (61.54: 17.95: 20.51), the C content of N-CDs greatly increased, which provides further evidence on the dehydration and carbonization of PPDI precursors in the solvothermal process [23,24]. In the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 2d–f), the C1s band consists of three main components: C–C/CC (284.7 eV), C–O/C–N (285.6 eV), and CO/CN (288.5 eV). In the case of N 1s, the peaks at 398, 399.5 and 400.5 eV confirm the presence of pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and graphitic N, respectively. The O1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.2 and 532.6 eV, corresponding to CO and C–O, respectively [2,25]. Above results illuminate that the CDs were doped by N element leading large conjugated sp2 domain core, and plentiful O and N atoms enriched the surface states, which indicates abundant functional groups on the surface of N-CDs, providing a possibility for the detection of water [2,6].

The optical properties of N-CDs were characterized by the UV–vis and PL spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the UV–vis spectrum exhibits three adsorption bands. The bands at about 325 and 400 nm are assigned to the n-π* transition of conjugated CO and CN, respectively, on the surface of N-CDs [6,18,26]. One more absorption band (peaked at ∼ 500 nm) is aroused by the n-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 system, indicating large sized conjugated sp2 domain in the N-CDs [9,13,18]. The optimal emission peak appears at 581 nm under 518 nm excitation. The PL excitation spectrum corresponds well to the UV–vis absorption peak at ∼500 nm, which implies that the orange fluorescence should derive from the large conjugated sp2 domain in the carbon cores. Furthermore, the PL excitation curve have only one peak, which manifests the uniformity of N-CDs in terms of their optical features [16]. Significantly, the self-absorption of N-CDs is very small because of the slight overlap between absorption and emission spectrum [13,27], which bestows efficient fluorescence emission upon N-CDs with an absolute PL QY of 38%. Strong orange luminescence of the CDs can be clearly observed under optimal excitation (inset of Fig. 3a). Fig. 3b displays the excitation-emission map of N-CDs. The typical excitation-independent PL emissions are observed. Specifically, the PL emission of N-CDs can be excited in a wide range of wavelengths from 450 to 550 nm, while the maximum emission peak position (581 nm) is insusceptible to this variation. The excitation-independent PL behavior is mainly connected to the homogeneous surface states of the CDs [10,27]. As a proof to this, pH-dependent PL behavior of N-CDs was also examined (Fig. S3). It can be seen that solution pH only affects the intensities but not the position of PL, and the PL intensities almost maintain constant in a wide range of pH values (3–13). Taken together, our CDs possess excellent and uniform optical properties, and their orange fluorescence is closely related to the conjugated sp2 domain in the carbon cores.

To further investigate the fluorescence origin and explore the formation process of N-CDs, control experiments were carried out with varied reaction time (1, 2, and 3 h). UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of these products are exhibited in Fig. 4 a and b. Compared to the starting material PPDI, the 1 h sample exhibits a weak absorption band located at 400 nm. When increasing the reaction time, this band becomes more distinct. Meanwhile, two new absorption peaks at 325 and 500 nm emerge, and their intensities improve slightly along with the reaction time varying from 2 to 3 h. Similarly, the PL emission intensities enhance gradually with increase in reaction time, and the corresponding QYs are measured in the inset of Fig. 4b. Although the QY of 1 h sample is very low, it provides evidence that the absorption peak at 400 nm is helpful for the orange fluorescence. That is, the surface states, which result from the introducing of nitrogen element, have some effect on the luminescence of N-CDs [16]. To this end, we argue that the orange emission of N-CDs is caused by both the core states and the surface states [2], particularly, the core states predominate in the effective orange luminescence. To further verify this hypothesis, time-resolved spectra of 1 and 3 h samples were probed in Fig. 4c. The decay curves of both samples can be fitted by a biexponential function, indicating that there exist two different emissive sites [23]. The short-lived τ1 and long-lived τ2 are the recombination from intrinsic state and defect state, respectively [28]. As shown, the τ1, τ2 and average lifetime for 3 h sample are all longer than that of 1 h sample, which means that with extending reaction time, nitrogen doping in CDs produced more luminescent centers [29]. Furthermore, the proportion of τ1 and τ2 change along with the increase of reaction time. It is clear that the long decay component becomes less prominent, while the faster channel rises in contribution during the synthesis. Taking into account the QYs of N-CDs, it can be concluded that the stronger luminescence originates directly from the increasing τ1 component. Namely, the improving core states dominate the optical features of N-CDs.

Fig. 4d presents a comparison of the FTIR spectra for the samples with different reaction time. The featured peak of ν (−NCO) at 2273 cm−1 for PPDI precursor disappeared after the solvothermal treatment, indicating that the isocyanate groups reacted with themselves once the experiment started. And besides, the disappearance of the bands at 1626 and 827 cm−1 corresponding to the ν (CC) and δ (C–H) from aromatic structure, after a reaction time of 1 h, can also confirm the consumption of PPDI precursor. The stretching vibration peaks of O–H and N–H are sharp and discrete for 1 h sample yet broad and integrated for 2 and 3 h samples. The broad absorption bands generally indicate more complex multiple structures on the CDs surfaces [16,30]. With increasing reaction time, both the peaks of CO/CN at 1668 cm−1 and C–N = at 1387 cm−1 become more and more pronounced, suggesting an increase in the content of the N-doped polyaromatic structures [2,25]. Surprisingly, the typical stretching vibration of CO at 1720 cm−1 emerges in the spectrum of 2 h sample, while it is blue-shifted with a more pronounced band at 1668 cm−1 for longer synthesis time. This implies that the carboxyl groups were generated at some time in the reaction, but then depleted through subsequent dehydration and carbonization processes. Based on the chemical structure of precursor PPDI and the experimental results, a possible mechanism for the formation of N-CDs is illustrated in Scheme 1 . As is well known, aromatic isocyanates are highly reactive. At the initial reaction stage, PPDI react with themselves following two types of chemical reactions: (1) self-polymerization reaction, producing uretdione dimer and isocyanurate trimer, and (2) self-condensation reaction, the carbodiimide reaction happens with releasing carbon dioxide gas [31,32], which may be a persuasive explanation for the dramatic decrease of oxygen content in the XPS result (Fig. 2c). Then the dimers, trimers, and carbodiimide structures can further react with PPDI, self-polymerize, or react with each other. In the next step, the polymer clusters are synthesized through intermolecular and intramolecular reactions among the obtained multimers, which agree well with the FTIR results (Fig. 4d). The appearance and later shift of the absorption peak at 1720 cm−1 indicate the formation and consumption of carbodiimide and multimerisation structures [25]. Consequently, the carbon cores are formed by dehydration of the polymer clusters, and finally the carbonized nanoparticles come into being [2,23].

The solvent effect of N-CDs was examined in various organic solvents and water. As shown in Fig. 5 , different PL peaks of the same N-CDs under 518 nm excitation are observed in different solvents. When dispersed in aprotic solvents, the CDs exhibit polarity-independent PL behavior with PL emission centered at about 580 nm, which denotes that the dipole moment of the solvents does not have much effect on the luminescence of N-CDs [4]. In contrast, when dispersed in protic solvents, such as methanol, ethanol, propanol, butanol, and water, the fluorescence of N-CDs show red shift, which could be attributed to the strong hydrogen-bond interactions between N-CDs and protic solvent [7]. The hydrogen-bond structure can construct robust bridge-like hydrogen-bonded networks, which not only cause a bigger Stokes’ shift resulting in the redshift of PL emission, but also restrict the structure of CDs leading to the enhancement of fluorescence [7,33]. Therefore, N-CDs in the alcohol solvents emit stronger fluorescence than in the aprotic solvents. Whereas in water, the fluorescence of the N-CDs is obviously quenched. In the light of this unique property, we exploited the obtained N-CDs as a good fluorescent probe for the detection of water in organic solvents.

Water as a common impurity in organic solvents, its qualitative and quantitative detection is very important for both academic and industrial applications [34]. Measuring water content can determine the purity of produced organic solvents, and then judge their eligibility. Moreover, in many chemical reactions, the presence of trace water could impede the activities of reactants, reduce the yield, and even cause dangerous situations [10]. Taking most PU as an example, water must be strictly prohibited, because the presence of trace water would consume a large amount of isocyanate reactants, which will change the ratio of raw materials and further affect the designed reaction, ultimately, the desired product could not be obtained. Thus, we assessed the capability of N-CDs for the detection of water in DMF and ethyl acetate, which are two common organic solvents in PU industry. Fig. 6 a displays the effect of water content in DMF on the emission spectra of the N-CDs sensor. With the increase of water content, the maximum emission peak shows stepwise red shift and the fluorescent intensity decreases gradually. The quenching efficiency F0/F exhibits a good linear relationship with water content in two different ranges (Fig. 6b), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of water, respectively.
F0/F = 0.0423[H2O] + 1.0042 (R2 = 0.9681, [H2O] = 0–1.5%)
F0/F = 0.0168[H2O] + 1.0373 (R2 = 0.9905, [H2O] = 1.5–25%)

More importantly, the F0/F is particularly sensitive when water content is less than 1.5%, and the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 0.0062% based on the three times standard deviation rule [12]. With regard to luminescent sensing, wavelength-based method as an alternative is more robust than intensity-based measurement [34]. Fig. 6c plots the emission peak of the N-CDs sensor versus water content in DMF. Similarly, the change of the characteristic peak can be roughly linearly fitted in two concentration ranges. The emission peak change, when water content is less than 1.5%, is exceedingly sensitive. A slight increase of water content can lead to a remarkable emission wavelength change. However, the emission wavelength is no longer noticeably dependent on the water content once it is higher than 15%. That means the emission wavelength of the N-CDs sensor could be potentially utilized for the detection of water content especially for less content.

A dynamic or static quenching models or sometimes both usually involve in the fluorescence quenching process [34,35]. From Fig. 6b, the experimental data are well fitted by the Stern-Volmer dynamic quenching equation (F0/F = KsvC + 1), where Ksv and C are the quenching constant and the concentration of quencher, respectively. Hence, we deduce that the interaction between the N-CDs sensor and the quencher water is possibly dynamic quenching. To further explore the quenching mechanism, the UV–vis absorption, FTIR and fluorescence lifetime experiments were carried out. Comparing the absorption spectra of N-CDs solution in absence and presence of water (Fig. 6d), the peak related to surface state at 400 nm is nearly disappeared due to the introduction of water. This evidently reveals the strong interaction between the N-CDs and H2O via the surface groups of N-CDs [12], which is further illustrated in Fig. 6e. Every vibration band is somewhat weakened, after adding water, except the N–H/O–H peak becomes stronger and broader. Accordingly, the proportion of component τ2 (Fig. 6f) also decreases from 75.81% to 51.64%. In addition, the interaction between N-CDs and water gives rise to the alteration of fluorescence lifetime. The lifetimes of N-CDs before and after adding water are 10.13 and 9.17 ns respectively, which further proves the mechanism of dynamic quenching rather than static quenching [35,36]. Therefore, a proposal should be made that the water-induced fluorescence quenching is presumably correlated with the surface state changes of N-CDs [10], which have roots in the combination of water and surface groups of N-CDs [37]. This lays the theoretical foundation for the application of N-CDs in water detection.

Subsequently, water determination can also be assessed in ethyl acetate, another common organic solvent for polyurethane. Similar PL spectral changes are depicted in Fig. S4 with slightly different sensitivity. The LOD is determined to be 0.0046%. Compared with other reported CDs sensors for water detection in organic solvents (Table 1 ), the N-CDs in this work exhibit a satisfactory detection performance for water. Thus, it is feasible to employ our N-CDs as a fluorescent probe to qualitatively and quantitatively detect water in organic solvents without any additional materials or sophisticated processes.

Another application of our orange-emitting N-CDs is to prepare CDs/polymer luminescent composites, which combine their advantages and become one of the most significant means to expand the application range of CDs [19,25]. The solid CDs/polymer composites were achieved by mixing the as-synthesized N-CDs with some organic polymers: PU, poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC), poly(vinyl pyrrolidone) (PVP), polycarbonate (PC), poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), and acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS). As displayed in Fig. S5a, the resulting CDs/polymer films exhibit similar emission peaks centered around 580 nm, which is the same as that occurred in different aprotic solvents (Fig. 5a). Among them, the flexible CDs/PU film not only shows the strongest fluorescence, but also owns the best processibility and film performance (Fig. S5b). Since PPDI, the starting material of N-CDs, is one of the common raw materials for PU, the CDs/PU composites present superior performance relying on the good compatibility between them [39]. In order to better disperse the CDs homogeneously in PU matrix, avoiding the aggregation as much as possible, we further compared in situ and ex situ fabrication of CDs/PU composite films. The concrete preparation processes are illustrated in Scheme S1. The same method was employed for synthesizing PU matrix. For in situ fabrication, the CDs were introduced before polymerization instead of simply blending them within the PU matrix. The digital photographs under daylight (Scheme S1and inset of Fig. 7 a) show better dispersion of the CDs, which brings about better transmittance, in the in situ films than in the ex situ composites (Fig. 7a). The emission peaks of both the obtained solid CDs/PU composites are comparable to that of the CDs solution without significant spectral shift (Fig. 7b). And both of the composites also exhibit excitation wavelength independent photoluminescence behavior as happened in solution (Figs. S6a and b). These results suggest that the embedded CDs retain their original fluorescence properties admirably. The QYs were measured to be 31% and 27% for in situ and ex situ composites respectively. The better quantum yield value may be attributed to the better dispersion of N-CDs in the former than in the later [7]. The UV–vis absorption and time-resolved spectra were also performed for comparison (Figs. S6c and d, respectively), and no obvious differences are observed. In short, the optical features in CDs solution can be easily switched to solid CDs/PU composites, especially for the in situ polymerization. Additionally, the CDs/PU film versus the CDs solution possesses better photo-stability under UV irradiation (Fig. S7), which is conducive to expending their practical application.

Moreover, we prepared a series of CDs/PU composites through in situ approach by adjusting the CDs concentration (see the Experimental section 2.3). As shown in Fig. 8 a, bare PU film, used as a reference, does not emit any luminescent signals. In contrast, CDs/PU films exhibit concentration dependent fluorescent intensity. The emission intensity gradually increases with the raise of CDs concentration, until the CDs solution was up to 9 mL. Meanwhile, the emission peak remains almost constant. Mechanical properties and TGA thermograms of pure PU and CDs/PU composite containing 9 mL CDs solution were tested in Fig. 8b and c, respectively. From the results, we witness an excellent enhancement of mechanical properties and slightly improved thermostability for CDs/PU composite compared with blank PU. And thus, our orange-emitting CDs/PU composites are potential engineered material for optoelectronic devices applications.

In summary, we synthesized novel orange emissive N-CDs by using PPDI as the only precursor material. The N-CDs exhibited the maximum emission at wavelength of 581 nm and possessed strictly excitation-independent behavior with an absolute PL QY as high as 38%. A detailed formation process for N-CDs from PPDI was proposed. Moreover, our N-CDs solution is highly sensitive to water and could be exploited as a fluorescent probe for water detection in organic solvents, which brings a promise in water determination using CDs during the quality control and production processes. Additionally, the obtained N-CDs can be homogeneously dispersed in PU matrix through in situ polymerization to fabricate CDs/PU composites. The PU matrix plays a vital role in providing mechanical support and uniform dispersion of the N-CDs. The facile preparation procedure and unique optical properties of N-CDs in both liquid and solid materials make promising application potentials in sensor and optoelectronic devices.

Jingjing Bai: with assistance from. Jingjing Cui: and. Yidan Ma: and. Wenhao Zhao: conducted the experiments and analyzed the results. Yulong Wang: suggestions about the experiments, and. Zhenzhong Li: supervised the study and designed the experiments, All authors contributed to the discussion and interpretation of the results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fund for the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (STIP, 2020L0654), Program for the Discipline Leaders of Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Shanxi ‘1331 Project’ Collaborative Innovation Center, and the Fund for Shanxi ‘1331 Project’ Key Subjects Construction.
